summary.intent: Address a third round of Codex review comments on PR #670 in the ars-ui repo, specifically fixing toggle group keyboard handling behavior and resolving all unresolved review threads before merging.
summary.outcome: Three Codex review threads were resolved by refactoring toggle group keydown logic to delegate activation to the native click path and restrict on_item_keydown to roving-focus navigation keys only. Code, spec, and tests were updated and committed as 'fix(components): refine toggle group key handling'.

repo: Toggle group items are real buttons, so Space/Enter activation should flow through the native click path — on_item_keydown should only handle roving-focus navigation keys (arrows, Home, End).
The project uses cargo xtask spec validate to cross-validate component specs against implementation.
Proptest state machine tests for toggle group exist and are marked #[ignored], run with PROPTEST_CASES=256 and --ignored flag.

CI pipeline has a rich set of gates: formatting, snapshot, workspace check, clippy, mutual-exclusion, unit, adapter, DOM/I18n browser, integration, adapter parity, feature core, error coverage, coverage, release verification, and feature-flag jobs.
Codex bot reviews PRs when triggered via '@codex review' comment and signals review status with emoji reactions (eyes = in progress).

code.finding: on_item_keydown previously handled Space/Enter activation; refactored to only translate roving-focus navigation keys, leaving activation to native button click behavior.
Second keydown handler site (likely for a variant mode) received the same activation-delegation refactor.
Spec wording updated to explicitly state that Space/Enter activation is handled by the native button event, not the group's keydown handler.
Additional spec sections updated to align with the new key-handling contract around roving focus navigation.

workflow: The loop pattern used here — trigger Codex review, poll for unresolved threads via GraphQL, patch, validate, push, repeat — is an effective autonomous PR-shepherding workflow.
GraphQL query against GitHub API is the reliable way to count unresolved Codex threads; REST reactions endpoint reveals review bot state (eyes reaction = active review).
Running targeted tests (cargo test -p ars-components toggle_group_keydown), then broader suite, then fmt check, then ignored proptests is the right incremental validation sequence before committing.
gh pr checks --watch --fail-fast is useful for CI gate monitoring but may time out on long-running pipelines, requiring fallback to sleep + manual poll.

friction: Multiple rounds of Codex review (at least 3 passes) were needed, each requiring a full CI run cycle of 10+ minutes before new threads appeared.
A typo in a GraphQL query (.data.repository.PullRequest vs .pullRequest) silently returned wrong results during one polling cycle.
Unit Tests took longer than prior runs in the third CI cycle, causing uncertainty about whether it had stalled or failed.
The contract disagreement about duplicate item registration required judgment on whether Codex's position was correct before patching — not a pure mechanical fix.
open_items: Duplicate item registration contract disagreement mentioned as a 'contract disagreement' — may need further discussion or a follow-up spec clarification if Codex flags it again.
CI coverage and release verification jobs were still running at the time of the last status update; their final results are not captured in this transcript.
Third Codex pass results were being acted on but the PR was not yet merged — merge readiness depends on CI completing green and Codex approving the latest commit.
